Video Title: MN 54: Potaliyasutta & Staying in the First Jhana | Daniel #13 | 05.11.24
Author: @MountainDharma
Comment:
For anyone who doubts Dhammarato’s interpretation 42:10 about the actual arising and passing of beings being a metaphor. The Buddha makes it clear in many places but here is one good example: 

"It would be better for the uninstructed run-of-the-mill person to hold to the body composed of the four great elements, rather than the mind, as the self. Why is that? Because this body composed of the four great elements is seen standing for a year, two years, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred years or more. But what's called 'mind,' 'intellect,' or 'consciousness' by day and by night arises as one thing and ceases as another. Just as a monkey, swinging through a forest wilderness, grabs a branch. Letting go of it, it grabs another branch. Letting go of that, it grabs another one. Letting go of that, it grabs another one. In the same way, what's called 'mind,' 'intellect,' or 'consciousness' by day and by night arises as one thing and ceases as another.

"The instructed disciple of the noble ones, [however,] attends carefully & appropriately right there at the dependent co-arising:

"'When this is, that is.

"'From the arising of this comes the arising of that.

"'When this isn't, that isn't.

"'From the cessation of this comes the cessation of that. (SN 12)

The same goes for the world:

[When this was said, the Blessed One responded:] "I tell you, friend, that it is not possible by traveling to know or see or reach a far end of the cosmos where one does not take birth, age, die, pass away, or reappear. But at the same time, I tell you that there is no making an end of suffering & stress without reaching the end of the cosmos. Yet it is just within this fathom-long body, with its perception & intellect, that I declare that there is the cosmos, the origination of the cosmos, the cessation of the cosmos, and the path of practice leading to the cessation of the cosmos."


It's not to be reached by traveling,
		the end of the cosmos —
		regardless.
And it's not without reaching
	the end of the cosmos
	that there is release
	from suffering & stress.

So, truly, the wise one,
an expert with regard to the cosmos,
a knower of the end of the cosmos,
having fulfilled the holy life,
	calmed,
knowing the cosmos' end,
	doesn't long for this cosmos
		or for any other. (AN 4.45)

“Mind” “self” “world” “beings” these are conventions of language, concepts, they all arise and pass away right here in experience. Dependent origination is APPARENT HERE AND NOW. It’s all in your head. If you want to be free, be free right now by following the summary of all Dhammas “nothing whatsoever should be clung to”. Why isn’t this enough? Because you are not satisfied. Why do you need more, more information, more experiences, more insights, more Jhanas and attainments? Because YOU ARE NOT SATISFIED. This is the answer to all your questions.

Video Title: We Don't Need To Be Right | The Sangha UK #212 | Dhammadasa | 04.25.24
Author: @MountainDharma
Comment: 30:33 in response to the question about “just do nothing.” This is one of the biggest points people misunderstand. It’s not that we have to “do nothing.” That would be a rule that creates aversion to all doings. Rather, we realize there is nothing we need to do in order to be satisfied. We change the order. Instead of needing to get or do things to be satisfied. We start with satisfaction, then do whatever it is that we need to do while being satisfied. I’m currently working three jobs due to some temporary financial challenges. I have two kids. There is never a moment to actually do nothing. But I maintain an attitude of satisfaction, letting go of any wanting that may arise, as I calmly and happily go about my doings. The reason it’s so difficult for people to understand that satisfaction can come first, and that we don’t need dissatisfaction to motivate what we do, is simply because we have been doing the craving to get way our entire lives, in every culture, across all time. So as simple as this change in order sounds, every cell resists acceptance of it. Instead, we think we have to “do nothing” to be happy. How useless is that? Then we want to know how we motivate ourselves without that dissatisfaction, that wanting to get it do? That’s simple too: mindfulness and wisdom. We pay attention to our lives, and know with wisdom what needs to be done; and we do it. No dissatisfaction needed. Of course having too much to do and too much to maintain is more of that base dukkha, so we can use the mindfulness and wisdom to begin reducing the load where it is possible. But there’s another trap, don’t wait until the load is light to be satisfied. Be satisfied while patiently reducing the external load. The internal load can be lightened immediately. As Buddhadasa said, “Dhamma is duty. Duty is Dhamma.” Do work of all kinds with an attitude of satisfaction (everything is ok) and an attitude of friendship (everyone is a friend). In this way, the path is beautiful in the beginning, the middle, and the end.

Video Title: Stop Being on a Path | The Sangha UK #211 | 04.21.24
Author: @MountainDharma
Comment: 30:58 what I would point out here and add to Michael’s response is that the purpose of Buddha Dhamma is not anatta (nonself) the purpose of the Dhamma is dukkha Nirodha, and the perception of anatta is only for that end. We have over time become hung up on anatta as an end goal when it’s just one perception that helps us renounce craving and clinging, so that we can realize the cessation of dukkha.

Author: @whitenozze
Comment: 1:44:00 best part

Video Title: Don't Get it Right, Just Get it Happily | On the Porch with David B #2 | 03.30.24
Author: @MountainDharma
Comment: More gold at 1:11:40. What’s educational for all of us is when we Watch ourselves come up with all our reasons so many things are wrong, or need to be done, or needs to be fixed, or needs to be gained, and we believe our never ending list while we see another’s list as their own set of reasons to remain dissatisfied. At that point, we realize that we are all the same, we are all just listing reasons to remain dissatisfied when Dhammarato is sitting there telling us there is not one single legitimate reason to be dissatisfied. And the endless pivoting: “ok, you’re right, that list I just gave is ok, but how about this other list, and this other list?” It’s never ending. Just throw it all out and be satisfied. Nothing is wrong. Nothing is missing.

Video Title: Don't Get it Right, Just Get it Happily | On the Porch with David B #2 | 03.30.24
Author: @MountainDharma
Comment: Just after 20:30 Dhammarato delivers his core teaching and after a year of talking to him and watching these videos, I’m even more amazed at how subtle and profound it is that he can listen to all of us go back to our delusions over and over and over when we called him for directions out of it. It’s just amazing insight, compassion, patience, and conviction that Dhammarato has to listen to everyone’s well articulated BS and still have the ability to calmly direct us back to the only point: no matter what you are looking for, the only reason you are looking is because you are dissatisfied and seeking a cure to that dissatisfaction. Simply stop looking and be satisfied. Nothing is missing! 😂

Video Title: The Buddha’s Smile | Marcus #39 | 10.12.22
Author: @starshiptexas
Comment: 34:15 the "dry" method isn't to stop anything. You just note the things you notice. One of the goals is to notice them more quickly. The only way in which it is "stopping" is that often, for example, with thinking, once you've noted thinking the thinking has already stopped and you're back to being mindful rather than letting your brain run away. Mahasi method is very much not about trying to stop or control anything. It's about seeing clearly the way things are, the insight and changes come on their own, you will know what to do. I think it can be more powerful figuring things out on your own but good dhamma talks are wonderful as well. 🧡

Author: @dhammariver8492
Comment: 34:20 we don't live in a paradise. I like this teacher, don't get me wrong, I think he's great 👍 but I believe this particular teaching was wrong because the Buddha taught that dukkha is inherent in the object. It's not something that we impose on reality with our mind. Not seeing this is classified as "vipallasa" (hallucination, perversion of view). The world is not perfect as it is. It's actually the opposite. The world is incomplete and flawed and constantly breaking up. This is dukkha. The mind seeks to complete itself with attractive objects and keeps failing because the objects themselves are impermanent and unsatisfactory.

Video Title: Building Confidence Through Understanding | Joe #8 | 1.20.22
Author: @MountainDharma
Comment: The discussion on Nibbita (disenchantment) starting at 47:00 is deeply delightful. In fact, this has been a big dhamma addiction for me, going back into delusion just to have the joy of coming back out of it again, over and over. Been doing that since a very young age. The missing piece for me was being told, by Dhammarato, to be satisfied with what I have already come to know and understand. That just this amount of understanding is good enough. Satisfaction must follow every step or else it’s possible to go back into the craving/dissatisfaction origin point in dependent origination. Many of us dhamma lovers get in this cycle

Video Title: The only Teaching of The Buddha: Dukkha, Dukkha Nirodha | Anna #2 | 8.6.21
Author: @tonnsarv
Comment:
BTW:, in Anurādha Sutta  (SN 22:86): 

 “And so, Anurādha—when you can’t pin down the Tathāgata as a truth or reality even in the present life—is it proper for you to declare, ‘Friends, the Tathāgata—the supreme man, the superlative man, attainer of the superlative attainment—being described, is described otherwise than with these four positions: The Tathāgata exists after death, does not exist after death, both does & does not exist after death, neither exists nor does not exist after death’?”

“No, lord.”

“Very good, Anurādha. Very good. Both formerly & now, it is only stress that I describe, and the cessation of stress.”

Video Title: Dark Night of the Soul | Dan C #3
Author: @mihugong3153
Comment:
13:57 authentic Yoda laughter - the proof that Dhammarato is for real!!

Seriously: Very interesting take on the subject, thanks a lot! Interesting to see that you also support the notion that has been present in the TMI community, where I practice, that so-called "dry insight" can lead to rougher experiences. Where you seem to differ, at least as far as I understand, is that in the TMI community, the phenomenon is understood more as a psychological one - people who have much unresolved psychological issues when deep insight occurs are more likely do experience difficult experiences aka Dark Night... it's not so much connected to the theoretical framework / expecations one has.


Just my passing observation. I'm a humble practitioner and don't have first-hand experience with these issues, so I don't really have an opinion on this. Still, it is good to hear that I have a good chance to keep progressing on the path without definitely having to go through years of hell realm suffering, right? :)

Thanks again for your interesting answer. My very best wishes!
